sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 88 actions; a strong score needs about 36 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
All cells are now gray again! The game reset all cells to gray after I clicked the 4th edge center. This confirms that the game validates after all 4 edge centers are clicked, and if they don't all match the target pattern (green), it resets them to gray.
The problem is that after the first click, the bar position changed from green-painting to gray-painting. So the subsequent clicks painted gray instead of green.
Wait, but I just verified that after clicking at the bar position `Sb`, the bar position stayed the same and the cell became green. So why did the subsequent clicks paint gray?
Let me re-examine. After the first click (top-center), the bar was at `Sb` and the cell became green. After the second click (left-center), the bar was still at `Sb` and the cell became green. But then after the third and fourth clicks, all cells became gray.
Wait, looking at the output more carefully:
- After clicking left-center (55, 25): top-center (49,29) = N (green), left-center (54,24) = N (green)
- After clicking right-center (55, 35) and bottom-center (60, 30): all cells = g (gray)
So the third click (right-center) must have triggered the reset. But why?
Let me think about this differently. Maybe the game validates after clicking the 3rd edge center (not the 4th). Or maybe the game validates after clicking any edge center that completes a certain pattern.
Actually, looking at the purple pattern, it has dots at the 4 edge centers. So the goal is to paint all 4 edge centers green. But the game seems to reset when I click the 3rd or 4th edge center.
Wait, maybe the issue is that the bar position DOES change after clicking, but only after clicking a certain number of cells. Or maybe the bar position changes after clicking the 2nd cell.
Let me check the bar position after the second click. Looking at the output:
"Bar after click: SSSggggggggggggggggggggggggSbBBBBBBBBBBBB"
This was after clicking the left-center (2nd click). The bar was at `Sb`. Then I clicked the right-center and bottom-center, and all cells became gray.
So the bar position was `Sb` after the 2nd click, and I clicked 2 more cells. But the result was all gray. This suggests that the bar position changed after the 2nd click, or that clicking the 3rd cell triggered a validation.
Actually, I think the issue is that the bar position DOES change after each click, but I didn't check the bar position after the 3rd click. Let me try again, but this time check the bar position after each click.
Wait, but the output showed that after the 2nd click, the bar was still at `Sb`. So the bar position didn't change after the 2nd click. But then after the 3rd and 4th clicks, all cells became gray.
Let me try a different approach: click one cell at a time and check the bar position and cell colors after each click.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# The game reset all cells to gray after clicking the 4th edge center.
# I need to understand why. Let me try clicking one cell at a time and checking.

# First, check current bar position
ascii = current_frame.ascii
lines = ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Current bar: {lines[19][14:55]}")

# The bar is at 'Sb' (right end). This should paint green.
# Let me click one cell and check.
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 50, 'col': 30}])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] == 9:
        boundary = n['boundary']
        min_row = min(p[0] for p in boundary)
        min_col = min(p[1] for p in boundary)
        if 47 <= min_row <= 63:
            print(f"({min_row},{min_col}): color={n['color']}")

ascii = current_frame.ascii
lines = ascii.split('\n')
print(f"Bar after 1st click: {lines[19][14:55]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Current bar: SSSggggggggggggggggggggggSSbbBBBBBBBBBBBB
(49,24): color=g
(49,29): color=N
(49,34): color=g
(54,24): color=g
(54,29): color=g
(54,34): color=g
(59,24): color=g
(59,29): color=g
(59,34): color=g
Bar after 1st click: SSSggggggggggggggggggggggSSbbBBBBBBBBBBBB
